title: ‘The Odyssey’ Screenplay Is Now Available to Buy Online
description:
If you purchase an independently reviewed product or service through a link on our website, Variety may receive an affiliate commission.

	Christoph
body:
If you purchase an independently reviewed product or service through a link on our website, Variety may receive an affiliate commission.

	Christopher Nolan fans can now bring the filmmaker’s latest epic home — no 70mm projector required.

	Nolan’s complete script for the blockbuster mythological adventure is listed on Amazon, where it’s already a No. 1 bestseller. Published by Faber & Faber, the 240-page paperback will officially release on Aug. 4 and is currently available for preorder.

		

	

	
					
		

					The Odyssey: The Official Screenplay		

			
							
	$13.95


	$17.95

																							
	
		
	

	The book offers readers a closer look at how Nolan adapted Homer’s foundational Greek poem for the screen, including the film’s dialogue and scene descriptions.


	“The Odyssey” stars Matt Damon as Odysseus, the king of Ithaca, and chronicles his perilous voyage home after the Trojan War. Damon leads the sprawling ensemble of Tom Holland, Anne Hathaway, Robert Pattinson, Lupita Nyong’o, Zendaya, Charlize Theron, Elliot Page, Jon Bernthal and John Leguizamo. The film has ignited the box office since its release, collecting $124.5 million domestically and $264.1 million worldwide over the weekend.  Written and directed by Nolan, it’s also one of the best-reviewed films of the year, with an “A” grade on CinemaScore exit polls and 95% from critics on Rotten Tomatoes.

	Interest in the movie has also extended well beyond the box office. Sales of Homer’s “The Odyssey” across its numerous translations and editions have reportedly risen 76% year to date, while audiobook listening surged around the film’s debut. The screenplay, already a No. 1 bestseller on Amazon, follows the trend. 

	The release continues a tradition of publishing Nolan’s scripts alongside his movies. Screenplay editions have previously been issued for  “Oppenheimer,” “Tenet,” “Interstellar,” “Inception” and “The Dark Knight Trilogy.”

	Pre-order Nolan’s official “Odyssey” screenplay here.

schema.headline: ‘The Odyssey’ Screenplay Is Now Available to Buy Online

title: ‘Doctor Strange’ Director Says Elon Musk ‘Doesn’t Know S— About Cinema’ After Musk Vows to Make AI ‘Odyssey’: ‘His Opinions on Art Are Worthless’
description:
Jul 22, 2026 3:10pm PT	
	

	
		‘Doctor Strange’ Director Says Elon Musk ‘Doesn’t Know S— About Cinema’ After Musk Vows to Make AI ‘Ody
body:
Jul 22, 2026 3:10pm PT	
	

	
		‘Doctor Strange’ Director Says Elon Musk ‘Doesn’t Know S— About Cinema’ After Musk Vows to Make AI ‘Odyssey’: ‘His Opinions on Art Are Worthless’
	
	
			


			
			
	

									Getty Images
	

		
	
				
	Elon Musk’s crusade against Christopher Nolan’s “The Odyssey” has raised eyebrows in Hollywood after the newly minted trillionaire vowed that before the end of the year, his AI tool Grok “will make a full-length movie of The Odyssey that is historically accurate and true to the art of Homer.” Musk also shared a three-minute clip of an AI-generated scene adapted from the poem, which was manufactured by a user on Grok.

	One figure from the film community, “Doctor Strange” and “The Black Phone” director Scott Derrickson, pulled no punches when addressing Musk directly on social media. “Clearly @elonmusk doesn’t know shit about cinema & he stopped reading in his 20s,” Derrickson wrote on Musk’s social media platform, X. “His opinions on art are worthless.”
	

	The Tesla mogul has railed against Nolan’s “Odyssey” long before it was released due to his perception of certain “woke” casting decisions. In March, Musk wrote that “Chris Nolan is an anti-White racist” for casting Lupita Nyong’o in the role of Helen of Troy, asserting that the director made the decision because “he wants the awards.” Musk said “Chris Nolan has lost his integrity” and called the casting an “insult” to Homer. Musk also took aim at Elliott Page, who plays Sinon in “The Odyssey,” reposting comments that mocked the transgender actor’s masculinity.

	“The Odyssey” is not the first film Musk has criticized on X; he similarly mocked “Barbie” for its feminist themes and Disney’s “Snow White” reboot led by Rachel Zegler. The anti-woke backlash to “The Odyssey” didn’t seem to matter. The Matt Damon-starring epic opened to $264 million worldwide and continues to sell out screenings months in advance.


	
		More from Variety

schema.headline: ‘Doctor Strange’ Director Says Elon Musk ‘Doesn’t Know S— About Cinema’ After Musk Vows to Make AI ‘Odyssey’: ‘His Opinions on Art Are Worthless’

title: LISTEN: Reba McEntire Revels in 50 Years of Making Music and Season 3 of NBC’s ‘Happy’s Place’
description: On today’s episode of “Daily Variety” podcast, country music superstar Reba McEntire discusses celebrating her golden anniversary in music this year
body:
On today’s episode of “Daily Variety” podcast, country music superstar Reba McEntire discusses celebrating her golden anniversary in music this year with a series of EP releases that pair a newly recorded song with several vintage tunes. McEntire also weighs in on how she’s navigated a turbulent TV marketplace to get her NBC comedy “Happy’s Place” to a third season.

	McEntire credits her manager, Justin McIntosh, with the concept behind the EP releases, which began in April and will continue through the year via her longtime label, MCA. Next up is “Ring, Ring,” which bows on July 24. As part of the release plan, McEntire has assembled special playlists for fans that dive deeper into her vast catalog.
	

	“It’s genius to be able to bring a new song to the fans along with five songs that are in the catalog,” McEntire says. “I think it’s a genius idea because even when you are at a concert, you say, ‘OK, I’m gonna play you a new song. Everybody goes, oh yeah, but they want to hear familiarity. They want to hear songs where they can sing along and they know the words and it takes them back to a place the first time they heard it, or with their best friend or someone they love. So to prepare a brand new song with older songs that they already are familiar with and they love is genius. And I’m so glad to be a part of this project.”

	McEntire has pulled off a rare feat these days with a multi-camera comedy that has found traction with a broadcast TV audience. Her NBC sitcom “Happy’s Place” returns for Season 3 on Oct. 23. The show reunited McEntire with the team that produced her previous WB Network series, “Reba,” from 2001-2007. This time around, she co-stars with her husband, actor Rex Linn.
	

	“There is something about a four-camera TV show. It’s nostalgic. It takes you back. It takes me back to my childhood when I was watching four camera television shows and absolutely loving it. It’s funny. It’s family entertainment. It’s great writing, great actors. Rex and I love to be on the show together for one thing. And then getting to work again with [co-star[ Melissa Peterman and then my showrunners Kevin Abbott and our producers Mindy [Schultheis] and Michael [Hanel] — everybody on that whole team. They are family. And we treat them like family. And, everybody knows that when you walk on ‘Happy’s Place’ set, we’re going to have a good time and you’re going to be treated well.”

	Another project close to McEntire’s heart in recent years has been her investment in a retail and entertainment center in Atoka, Oklahoma, near her hometown of Chockie. It’s housed in a refurbished, 15,000 square foot building from 1907, and the complex includes a diner dubbed Reba’s Place. It’s infused with memories for McEntire and her siblings and a tribute to their mother, Jacqueline McEntire, who died in 2020 at age 93.

	“Reba’s Place is a restaurant that serves great family food. And then we also have a stage where we have live music. My brother and sister, Pake and Susie, have performed on the stage as I have also,” she says. “On the second floor is more dining and with a cutout of the floor where you can look down and see the stage. The third floor is the little store where you can buy all kind of goodies, t shirts to wooden spoons. And then we’ve got a little speakeasy in the back and then we’ve got a library. When Mama passed away March of 2020, she had so many books in her house, we didn’t know what to do with them. We all took a bunch, but then we decided to put them in the restaurant there in Atoka. And so that’s Jackie’s Library.”

	Listen to Daily Variety on iHeartPodcasts, Apple Podcasts, Variety’s YouTube Podcast channel, Amazon Music, Spotify and other podcast platforms.

schema.headline: LISTEN: Reba McEntire Revels in 50 Years of Making Music and Season 3 of NBC’s ‘Happy’s Place’

title: Yeon Sang-ho Returns to ‘Parasyte’ Universe With ‘Tamiya,’ Starring Shioli Kutsuna, at Netflix
description: Yeon Sang-ho is returning to the “Parasyte” universe with “Parasyte: Tamiya,” a new live-action series starring Shioli Kutsuna for Netflix, set to s
body:
Yeon Sang-ho is returning to the “Parasyte” universe with “Parasyte: Tamiya,” a new live-action series starring Shioli Kutsuna for Netflix, set to stream in 2027.

	The series marks another collaboration between Netflix and Yeon (“Hellbound,” “Parasyte: The Grey”), who developed “Parasyte: Tamiya” and co-wrote the script with Ryu Yong-jae. Wowpoint, the production company behind Yeon’s “Human Vapor” and “Parasyte: The Grey,” is producing. Kakimoto Kensaku (“Glass Heart”) and Nakagawa Kazuhiro (“Double,” “Bullet Train Explosion”) are directing.
	

	“Parasyte: The Grey” spent four weeks on Netflix’s Global Top 10 Non-English TV list and reached No. 1 in 34 countries following its 2024 release.


	In the original manga, Ryoko Tamiya is a parasite who takes over the body of a high school teacher, initially violent and indiscriminate before gradually learning from her human surroundings. Witnessing a parasite and human sharing a single body leads her to wonder whether coexistence might offer her species a better chance of survival than conflict. “Parasyte: Tamiya” tells the story from her perspective, tracking the original human, the parasite who inhabits her and the creature caught between both worlds.

	Kutsuna (“Sanctuary,” “Deadpool 2,” “Gundam”) plays Ryoko Tamiya across three registers: the original human, the parasite after it takes over and the parasite as it struggles to interact with humanity.

	Yeon said, “Her struggles over the nature of her own existence are what elevate the original ‘Parasyte’ from a simple hero-based manga aimed at kids to something deeper. I think the anguish she experiences is the essence of ‘Parasyte.'”
	

	Yeon was also asked about his choice of lead. “I’m sure that was a big challenge for Kutsuna as an actor,” he said. “I think she did a wonderful job of portraying the process of a non-human creature coming to understand both humanity and its own existence.”

	On the choice of directors, Yeon said, “I chose these two directors precisely because they have such different styles, and I had high hopes for a collaboration between them.”

	Kutsuna said, “Ryoko Tamiya is the most challenging role I have ever played in my acting career. This series was brought to life through the dedication of the entire cast and crew, who supported one another through every challenge along the way.”

	Netflix Japan’s Sato Yoshihiro, manager of live action creative, added, “‘What makes a human?’ ‘Can we coexist with beings that are different from us?’ These questions posed by ‘Parasyte’ more than 30 years ago feel much more pressing for our modern society that has placed non-sentient AI at all of our fingertips.”

	The “Parasyte” manga, created by Hitoshi Iwaaki and published by Kodansha, has previously been adapted into a film series, an anime and the Korean drama “Parasyte: The Grey.”

schema.headline: Yeon Sang-ho Returns to ‘Parasyte’ Universe With ‘Tamiya,’ Starring Shioli Kutsuna, at Netflix

title: Where to Find a Commonwealth Games 2026 Livestream in the U.S. to Watch the Event for Free
description:
If you purchase an independently reviewed product or service through a link on our website, Variety may receive an affiliate commission.

	Some of t
body:
If you purchase an independently reviewed product or service through a link on our website, Variety may receive an affiliate commission.

	Some of the best summer athletes in the world are heading to Scotland for the 2026 Commonwealth Games.

	A quadrennial event dating back to 1930, the Commonwealth Games unites thousands of athletes from more than 70 nations across the British Commonwealth — I.e. countries that are currently or were formerly part of the British Empire. So while countries like Canada, Australia, England, New Zealand, South Africa and Jamaica will all be participating, there will not be a U.S. contingent at the Games.


	This year’s Commonwealth Games run from July 23 to August 2 in Glasgow, Scotland, with events airing live and as nightly highlights on channels like 7Plus in Australia and Channel 5 in the UK.

	North American viewers can watch the Games for free on CBC, Canada’s national public broadcaster, via a virtual private network like ExpressVPN or NordVPN (NordVPN excludes a 30-day money back guarantee). Your VPN lets you set your location to Canada to stream the CBC’s coverage of the 2026 Commonwealth Games online from anywhere in the world.

	Or set your location to Australia or the United Kingdom to stream the 7Plus and Channel 5 Commonwealth Games feed online.
	
		How to Watch the 2026 Commonwealth Games From the U.S.	

	You can also watch the Commonwealth Games in the U.S. through beIN SPORTS, the Qatar-based sports media group that is now available in America on streaming services like Fubo and Sling. 

	Fubo offers beIN SPORTS on its own for just $5.99/month, or as part of the Fubo Core package, which gets you 200 live TV channels to stream online for $63.99/month (regularly $88.99 monthly).


	Don’t have Fubo? Test drive the service with a five-day free trial here and use it to stream the Commonwealth Games in the U.S. for free. Fubo’s plans include free DVR so you can record the events to watch back on-demand whenever you want.

	The cheapest way to get beIN SPORTS on Sling, meantime, is to sign up for the streamer’s Sling Orange base plan (from $45.99/month) and then add the Sports Extra package for an additional $11 a month. You’ll get more than 40 live TV channels in total that you can stream online.

	Sling Orange includes 50 hours of free cloud DVR in addition to an on-demand library of free TV shows and movies that you can stream online.
	
		Commonwealth Games 2026 Sports	

	Unlike previous Commonwealth Games, which have featured more than 30 different summer sports, this year’s event will include just 10 sports, the lowest number of events offered at the Games since 1994. Organizers say rising costs, lack of venues and viewership decline have led to the removal of sports like field hockey, diving, rugby sevens and cricket. 

	Still, there’s plenty of big names to watch for in the remaining sports, which include athletics, swimming, artistic gymnastics, boxing and cycling. Paris 2024 Olympic swimming champions Kaylee McKeown from Australia and Adam Peaty from England, are expected to compete, as is Canada’s gold-medal winning 4×100 meter men’s relay team in track and field.

	The track event will also see Scottish middle-distance runner Josh Kerr, who just broke the 27-year world record in the mile, and Jamaican superstar Shericka Jackson, a two-time World Champion who holds the second-fastest time ever recorded in the women’s 200m events.

	Reigning Olympic pommel horse champion Rhys McClenaghan from Northern Ireland will compete in the men’s gymnastics event, where he’ll face off against Cyprus’ Marios Georgiou, who is a former European All-Around champion and a three-time Olympian.

	This year’s Commonwealth Games kicks off with an Opening Ceremony featuring British singer-songwriter Tom Walker (of “Leave the Light On” fame), along with Scottish singer-songwriter, KT Tunstall and other local artists. The last edition of the Commonwealth Games was held in 2022 in Birmingham, where rockers Duran Duran performed at the Opening Ceremony.

schema.headline: Where to Find a Commonwealth Games 2026 Livestream in the U.S. to Watch the Event for Free

title: Olivia Wilde Slams Elon Musk as a ‘Creature’ and Says ‘I Deeply Resent Him Now,’ Years After Meeting at SpaceX Headquarters
description: Olivia Wilde recently appeared on “The Louis Theroux Podcast” and slammed Elon Musk while also speaking out against misogynistic incel culture at la
body:
Olivia Wilde recently appeared on “The Louis Theroux Podcast” and slammed Elon Musk while also speaking out against misogynistic incel culture at large. “The Invite” director and star revealed she once toured Musk’s SpaceX headquarters and met the business tycoon long “before he turned into a creature.” 

	“I really resent him now. I really deeply resent him,” Wilde said. “I met him many, many years ago. I think SpaceX had just built their headquarters in L.A. and I got to go on a tour because I wanted to see the rocket ships. He was interested in donating money to an organization that I was a part of in Haiti that was building hospitals and schools. I believe he did donate a little bit of money, and that’s appreciated. I was confused by him. I would never have predicted he would go down the path he has gone down. He did seem really smart.”
	

	Wilde noted how Musk has perpetuated some of incel culture’s more disturbing ideology, particularly the belief that men are more powerful than nature. The filmmaker noted how Musk connects to this mentality by “talking about how we don’t have enough people and the world is underpopulated and we could handle another billion people.” Musk often expresses his belief that humanity is facing an impending “underpopulation crisis,” arguing it’s a bigger threat than global warming.


	During the press tour for “Don’t Worry Darling” in fall 2022, Wilde garnered the attention of incel culture when she said she based Chris Pine’s villain character off author and commentator Jordan Peterson, who Wilde referred to as “this pseudo-intellectual hero to the incel community.” Peterson then broke down in tears during an interview with Piers Morgan in reaction to Wilde’s statement. 
	

	Wilde defined incels at the time as “disenfranchised, mostly white men, who believe they are entitled to sex from women. And they believe that society has now robbed them — that the idea of feminism is working against nature, and that we must be put back into the correct place.”

	Speaking now on Theroux’s podcast, Wilde admitted that it “might have ben unfair for me to call [Peterson] a pseudo-intellectual” because “he obviously thinks about things deeply, but I just feel that he operates in a way that is a buttoned-up version of the type of thinkers that lead to the Andrew Tate’s of the world. There are different layers to that community, some of whom speak more clearly the points they are all actually filtering through.”

	“My issue with Peterson is a lot of people who are not in any way misogynist have adopted this sanitized version of the same philosophy that’s rooted in deep misogyny and weaponized by the incel community,” Wilde continued. “The basic point of it is entitlement to power. That is something that many men are struggling with… I was disturbed by what I saw as this misogyny that was being weaponized and leading us to elect Donald Trump twice and beyond that just empower these groups of people who I think are dangerous.”

	When asked in 2022 about why Wilde’s comment made him so emotional, Peterson fought back tears and said, “It’s really something to see — constantly how many people are dying for lack of an encouraging word and how easy it is to provide that if you’re careful.” 

	Wilde told Theroux she was more or less dumbfounded by this comment, explaining: “‘Lack of an encouraging word’ is so absurd. There is a large, booming word of encouragement happening at all time for these men, which is: ‘This world is yours and you are entitled to it.’ That’s deeply ingrained in our society. This idea they need a reminder of encouragement and deserve more is ludicrous.”

	Listen to Wilde’s full interview on “The Louis Theroux Podcast” here.

schema.headline: Olivia Wilde Slams Elon Musk as a ‘Creature’ and Says ‘I Deeply Resent Him Now,’ Years After Meeting at SpaceX Headquarters

title: ‘Star Trek: Strange New Worlds’ Cast, Co-Showrunner Talk Playing With Genres in Season 4, ‘Joyful Curiosity,’ and That Puppet Episode
description: The team behind “Star Trek: Strange New Worlds” took the stage at 92NY in New York on Tuesday to delve into what fans can expect from Season 4 of th
body:
The team behind “Star Trek: Strange New Worlds” took the stage at 92NY in New York on Tuesday to delve into what fans can expect from Season 4 of the Paramount+ series. 

	Series stars Anson Mount (Capt. Pike), Celia Rose Gooding (Ensign Uhura), Melissa Navia (Lt. Ortegas), and Carol Kane (Cmmdr. Pelia) were joined by co-showrunner Akiva Goldsman in a conversation moderated by Variety‘s Joe Otterson. The full conversation can be found below. 
	

	After fans got a special sneak peak at the Season 4 premiere, Goldsman said that Season 4 features episodes that play across a number of genres (similar to how the show pulled off that musical episode in Season 2). 


	“There’s a cadence between kind of classic ‘TOS’ sci-fi thriller and then the thing that actually makes us ‘Strange New Worlds’…which is our worlds are genres,” Goldsman said. “Those are our strange new worlds. It’s the lens that allows us to tell stories in a way that typically, or at least consistently, hasn’t been done in ‘Star Trek’ before.” 

	Mount said that it was a remarkable experience to have Goldsman and fellow showrunner Henry Alonso Myers ask the cast what kinds of genres they’ve always wanted to act in and then actually let them do it. 

	“It got to the point where we went from ‘What can they do?’ to ‘What can we give them they can’t do?'” Goldsman said. “And it became a kind of extraordinary circus of the imagination, and everybody was game.” 

	And while Goldsman said he did not want to spend too much time talking about the show’s upcoming final season, he promised that it delivers a “more singular” and thorough goodbye to the show for fans. 
	

	But speaking of genres, it has already been revealed that this season will feature an episode in which the crew of the Enterprise are transformed into puppets. Yes, puppets, made by none other than the iconic Jim Henson’s Creature Shop. Gooding said getting to use the puppets was “such a gift as someone who grew up watching all of the puppets that came out of that studio.

	“To have our characters immortalized in that same way. It’s wacky, silly, but also incredibly serious and heartwarming,” she said. “It’s so nostalgic but futuristic, which is what ‘Strange New Worlds’ has done from Season 1.” 

	All onstage also agreed that turning Pelia into a puppet was largely “redundant.” 

	Speaking of Kane, a living legend across TV and film, she had famously said upon joining the show that she had seen no “Star Trek” series or movies prior to joining “Strange New Worlds.” When asked if that has changed, she carefully said, “I’ve seen…some, but not all. But enough to get a sense of the fact that I’m extraordinarily lucky to be a part of this world.”

	“People really care about the tales being told,” she said. “And the tales being told all have hope at the base of them and it’s so hard in our modern world to feel hope. So I’m so grateful to be part of something that — it’s not easy, it’s not pretty, blah blah blah, but there is some hope and heart as the foundation. Because there’s no future without that.” 

	Mount and Gooding also touched on the fact that there characters are well-established in “Star Trek” lore, but yet they and the writers are still trying to break new ground so as to not simply retread what was done with them before. 

	“What’s weird about the character is there’s so little of him but he’s so revered in canon,” Mount said of Pike. “But it was great being able to come in and a weird challenge. I’ve never had a character where we know the first act, we know the third act, but not the second act. And I got to come in and flesh that out and it’s been a tremendous amount of fun.”

	Gooding again paid tribute to original “Star Trek” cast member Nichelle Nichols, who played Uhura across three seasons of the original show and six movies. “We, as a loving audience, know who Uhura is going to become but she doesn’t and we get to watch her find out. And we get to watch her have these canon experiences that establish her as the person she’s going to become and my job was just to fill in the blanks.” 
	

	Then taking questions from the audience, the cast were asked what quality of their characters they would like to see more of in the world. 

	Navia said that she wishes to see more love of aviation and exploration, and that one of her joys working on the show is getting to meet real pilots or those who have become inspired to be pilots after watching. 

	“I’ve started to dive really deep into the history of aviation and we get to be in collaboration a lot with members of the space community,” she said. “I’ve gotten to work with NASA. I’ve gotten to do some very cool things and when you look at the way aviation started, it was all about people who were taking risks. They were taking risks and collaborating…We were actually talking about this the other day, our friend, an astronaut, Dr. Sian Proctor, she always says, ‘When you solve for space, you solve for Earth.'”

	Gooding said she would like to see more “joyous curiosity.” 

	I think Uhura has a really beautiful way of embracing the unknown with joy and curiosity as opposed to fear and division,” she said. “I think we need a little more joyous curiosity in the world right now.” 

	“Star Trek: Strange New Worlds” Season 4 premieres July 23 on Paramount+ with new episodes dropping weekly.

schema.headline: ‘Star Trek: Strange New Worlds’ Cast, Co-Showrunner Talk Playing With Genres in Season 4, ‘Joyful Curiosity,’ and That Puppet Episode

title: Hello Sunshine’s Head of TV and Film on 10 Years of Reese Witherspoon’s Production Company, ‘Big Little Lies’ Season 3, Apple’s ‘Lucky’ and More ‘Elle’
description: Lauren Neustadter and Reese Witherspoon are having a big summer, with back-to-back high-profile releases: July’s debuts of “Legally Blonde” prequel
body:
Lauren Neustadter and Reese Witherspoon are having a big summer, with back-to-back high-profile releases: July’s debuts of “Legally Blonde” prequel series “Elle” on Amazon’s Prime Video and the Anya Taylor-Joy-led action miniseries “Lucky” at Apple TV.

	Premiering just two weeks apart, both shows are produced by Witherspoon’s media company, Hello Sunshine, which is coming up on its 10th anniversary this fall. And up next, the production company behind “Daisy Jones & the Six” and “Where the Crawdads Sing” has HBO’s “Big Little Lies” Season 3, the release of Sony Pictures’ “The Nightingale,” starring Dakota Fanning and Elle Fanning, and the second season of “Elle” (which has already wrapped filming) to look forward to in 2027.
	

	It’s a packed slate that’s the culmination of nearly a decade of work from Witherspoon and the Hello Sunshine team, which is led by president and head of TV and film Neustadter.




	

					Lauren Neustadter and Reese Witherspoon at Prime Video’s “Elle” New York Premiere on June 23, 2026 in New York, New York.
									Kristina Bumphrey/Variety
	



	“At the very beginning, a part of the mission was to create opportunities for women to shine, and creating opportunities for them to shine in front of the camera, behind the camera, as members of our team,” Neustadter tells Variety. “It was all equally important to us, and it was really a core part of the mission of Hello Sunshine when we talked about, ‘What do we want this company to be?’ Whether it’s scripts or books, whether it’s as directors, whether it’s as heads of department on our production.”

	From “The Morning Show” to “Little Fires Everywhere,” Neustadter says she and Witherspoon have been focused on finding “exactly the right people to bring these projects to life,” including Jonathan Tropper for “Lucky” and “Elle” showrunners Laura Kittrell and Caroline Dries.
	

	Neustadter says this partner-focused strategy has led to success for Hello Sunshine, as well as prepared the team for even bigger projects ahead, including the return of “Big Little Lies,” which premiered back in 2017.



	

					Anya Taylor-Joe and Lauren Neustadter
									Michael Buckner/Variety
	



	“The reality is, Hello Sunshine didn’t exist when the first season of ‘Big Little Lies’ was made. That was Reese and Bruna [Papandrea] and Pacific Standard and David E. Kelley, and they did the most amazing job ever,” Neustadter says. “And we were privileged to be invited in for the second season, and also for the third. It is the most inspired and inspiring team, and I think there are so many things about that show that are extraordinary and special. And one of them is it is a true team effort.”

	The third season of “Big Little Lies” will be based on author Liane Moriarity’s upcoming sequel book, “Big Little Truths,” and features the returns of Witherspoon, Nicole Kidman, Shailene Woodley and Laura Dern, among others.

	“HBO is the most amazing partner alongside this incredible producing team, and I think we all care so much about every single one of these amazing characters,” Neustadter says. “So just being in the conversation and thinking about what this next chapter looks like for these extraordinary women in Monterey, it’s a privilege. But it is truly, truly a team effort and I know Season 3 is going to be incredible.”

	Read more from Variety‘s deep-dive interview with Neustadter below.
	
		Hello Sunshine has plenty of experience with book-to-screen adaptations. How are you approaching the development process for “Big Little Lies” Season 3, given that “Big Little Lies” Season 2 went off book and continued beyond the original source material, but now Season 3 will be based on the upcoming sequel, “Big Little Truths”?	

	One of the things that we try to do with every single project is make sure that everyone involved loves it like we love it. “Big Little Lies” is an incredible example of that, because I think everyone cares incredibly deeply about these characters. And so we are 100% immersed in the book, and in thoughtful conversation about the adaptation and about the characters. You also have the gift of having actresses who know and love and have lived these characters, who are in the conversation about how you’re taking it from book to screen, which is always such an amazing privilege. I think it just makes the material so very rich. So I’m very excited about everything that’s happening and all the conversations that are going on.
	
	
		What does the next year look like for Hello Sunshine? What are you going into production on next?	

	We are excited about “Big Little Lies.” I’m hoping that we’ll be in production on a third season of “Elle,” and the second season of “Elle” will come out. The fifth season of “The Morning Show” will come out. “The Nightingale” will come out, and then we have a couple of other movies that we’re gearing up on that will hopefully be in production in ’27. So it should be busy.
	
		“Lucky” is a different project for Hello Sunshine, being an action-heavy show. Did that require things that previous shows and movies hadn’t?	

	It feels like with the landscape of each of these episodes you’re with her as she’s on the run. You’re seeing her as a chameleon in a way that I think is so fascinating and intriguing, and actually quite singular in a limited series like this. We fell in love with this book, and really, really with this character, and also with the complicated relationship that she had with her father.

	We always love to stretch. We always love to go into an area that we haven’t been in before, and explore, and be curious, and bring these characters to life, but also team up with people who have very specific superpowers. So Jonathan Tropper was that person. He’s a person that we admired greatly, both as a screenwriter and as an author. He can do this full-throttle action in a way that, quite frankly, was new to us. So we knew that we needed an expert in that field to come in and architect these badass sequences and think really big about what this could be. It was incredibly important to us, to Hello Sunshine, to the [future] readers of the book, that it would be right because the book had not yet published. We needed to protect the heart and soul and the vulnerability of this character, and so we needed a creator of the series that was going to do both.

	Even at the end, when we’re sitting in the mix and you’re calibrating sound, and I’m thinking about, how is Lucky breathing? And how are we emotionally with her? And Jonathan is like, “Can we make the gunshot a little louder? This explosion, I want to really feel it.” And it was just awesome. And when I think about “Daisy Jones & the Six,” it was similar on the music side.
	
		What have you thought of the response to “Elle” from existing “Legally Blonde” fans?	

	It’s everything that I hoped for. I feel like women are just coming up to me and saying thank you for making a joyful show. And of the things that Reese really acutely felt was that, particularly for a younger generation of women, the messaging they were receiving on social media was not necessarily telling them they were wonderful and perfect just the way they are and capable of anything they dreamed of. I think, without being treacly, “Elle” really reminds us that we’re good enough, and it’s OK to be different. And I think Elle herself embodies that. I also think [her mother] Eva [played by June Diane Raphael] really embodies that.
	

	One of the things that I love, and it’s been lovely and wonderful to see the response to Eva’s character, who we architected very intentionally as a character that was a bit of a love letter to those of us that saw the movie in the theaters when it came out, and now are in a different phase of our lives. And I think Eva is, for us, a woman who is grappling with a different set of issues and asking questions about identity when she’s in her 40s and she’s moving to a different place. 

	One of the things that we’re always trying to do is connect with women and reflect their lived experience. And hopefully “Elle” does that in a joyful way. In a way, I think it’s a show that women can relax and enjoy. We just wanted to give the audience and the fans of “Legally Blonde” something delicious and something that they could connect to. And I hope we’ve done it. A person that I went to high school with, who’s now living in North Carolina, texted and said, “My daughter is obsessed with the show, and we’re savoring it, and we’re watching one episode every night together.”
	
		“Elle” has already wrapped production on its second season. How did you make the choice to go right into Season 2 before Season 1 aired, knowing there wouldn’t be that audience feedback to use?	

	We wrapped Season 2 before we had our press and premiere around Season 1. And a lot of that, to be very honest with you, had to do with weather, because one of the things that was challenging was there was a lot of sunshine in Season 1, and we needed to calibrate that with visual effects. And we were really trying to feel Seattle. And we recognized that if we waited a typical period, we would be looking at a summer start, and so then it’s going to be glorious. You’re not going to get the gloomy skies. You’re not going to get the rain. You’re not going to get the elements. So one, it’s a testament to Amazon’s belief in the show and enthusiasm, and as they were seeing cuts, I think they were feeling really excited.

	And we figured, let’s go, let’s keep this momentum up. We knew that we wanted to do each season as a semester for “Elle,” so we’re starting with the first semester of her junior year, and then we’re rolling right into the second. It made a lot of sense in terms of story and momentum to continue. I think the show really benefited from not having a huge amount of downtime. I think emotionally, everybody was very connected to where their character was at the end of Season 1 as we go into Season 2.
	
	
		How do you balance the workload with so many ongoing and new projects? How involved is Reese on a day to day basis when she’s starring in some of these and not in others?	

	We love to be busy. It’s something that we have in common, and also we just have the most amazing team. I admire so many things about Reese, but one of the things that I admire most is she’s very intentional about hiring people who are excellent at what they do, and then trusting them to do their jobs. So she’s an amazing partner. She is certainly involved in everything. She’s reading all the books. She’s reading all the scripts. She’s watching all the cuts. She’s giving us her thoughts. 

	At the same time, if we are moving at a fast clip, I will touch base with her and say, I want to let you know this is what’s going on and I feel great about it, and she’s like, “I feel great about it too,” and we go. Particularly, in a moment like this, she’s starring in “The Morning Show,” and we’re launching two shows, and we’re optioning a new book that we’re so excited about. We are trying to juggle all of these things elegantly. And I think trust is a very important piece of it. I will say, an equally important piece of it is having an unbelievable team. We have people who’ve started off as assistants who are now running sets. They help us to make it all happen, and it’s so exciting just to think about everything that we’ve accomplished in the past decade or so.

	This interview has been edited and condensed.

schema.headline: Hello Sunshine’s Head of TV and Film on 10 Years of Reese Witherspoon’s Production Company, ‘Big Little Lies’ Season 3, Apple’s ‘Lucky’ and More ‘Elle’

title: Darren Aronofsky’s AI Studio Primordial Soup Raising $15 Million
description:
Darren Aronofsky has showcased the work his new AI-forward studio Primordial Soup can do. Now he wants more money to do it.

	The company disclosed
body:
Darren Aronofsky has showcased the work his new AI-forward studio Primordial Soup can do. Now he wants more money to do it.

	The company disclosed in a Securities and Exchange Commission filing last week that it was seeking to raise $15 million in cash in exchange for equity in the studio. The filing lists Aronofsky and his producing partner Dylan Golden as chief executives in the venture, and it stated that Primordial Soup has so far sold just over $11 million in equity this month.
	

	A spokesperson for Aronofsky did not immediately respond to a request for comment.


	The effort underscores Aronofsky’s ambitions in the space, with the “Requiem for a Dream” and “Black Swan” director’s studio hiring for a “Head of Studio” and multiple “Generative Artists” to work on AI-focused projects. Primordial Soup launched a year ago and partnered with Google’s DeepMind unit to produce multiple short films, with each filmmaker receiving mentorship by Aronofsky and the DeepMind team. 

	“Filmmaking has always been driven by technology,” Aronofsky said in a statement at the time. “After the Lumiere brothers and Edison’s ground-breaking invention, filmmakers unleashed the hidden storytelling power of cameras. Later technological breakthroughs — sound, color, VFX — allowed us to tell stories in ways that couldn’t be told before. Today is no different. Now is the moment to explore these new tools and shape them for the future of storytelling.”

	Since the launch, Primordial Soup developed animated series “On This Day… 1776,” which used a hybrid model of AI tools and traditional filmmaking methods. to recreate moments throughout the year America was founded.

schema.headline: Darren Aronofsky’s AI Studio Primordial Soup Raising $15 Million

title: Shaken, Soaked and Seasick: What It’s Like to Survive ‘The Odyssey’ in 4DX
description: There’s no question that seeing Christopher Nolan’s epic “The Odyssey” in 70mm and Imax – or both – is an expansive and breathtaking experience. But
body:
There’s no question that seeing Christopher Nolan’s epic “The Odyssey” in 70mm and Imax – or both – is an expansive and breathtaking experience. But for cinephiles who have already checked off those formats on their list, what’s it like to watch the Matt Damon-starring, three-hour blockbuster in 4DX?

	The seat-rumbling, water-spritzing technology, available in nearly 800 movie theaters around the world, promises a “fully immersive cinematic experience that makes you feel like you’re in the movie.” But does it really? Three intrepid Variety writers – two who had seen the movie before and one first-timer – set out on an odyssey to find out. The three sailed into Regal’s L.A. Live cinemas and were fortunately better-provisioned than Damon and his exhausted crew. Tanya stockpiled mozzarella sticks and Gatorade, while Pat smuggled in a peanut butter protein bar and a bottle of water for the three-hour tour.
	


	Pat was about to put in some eyedrops to prep for the long running time when the moviegoers suddenly embarked on the first stop of the epic journey: the trailer for 4DX. The seats heaved and jiggled, we were spritzed with water, we were shaken and stirred and primed for equally bouncy trailers for “Spider-Man: Brand New Day” and “The Fast and the Furious” rerelease. The onslaught during the pitch for enlisting in the Navy was particularly jarring.

	Then the film began. Read on to see who survived the hero’s journey.



	Pat 

	The main selling points for 4DX are shaking seats and spraying water, with a little wind thrown in. The breeze sometimes felt like the person sitting behind me was ruffling my hair – but since it was my own co-worker, that seemed unlikely. During the many scenes of roiling seas and inevitable shipwrecks, the shaking met the action appropriately. Did it add to the experience? Kind of — we could certainly empathize with how battered and bruised Odysseus and his men must have felt after all those cursed voyages.


	The water was not as bad as I had feared – I had prepared by bringing a hoodie in case of a full Sea World-esque splash zone, but the light spritzing was fairly refreshing. Nonetheless, midway through the film I took advantage of the “turn water off” button to enjoy Nolan’s ambitious filmmaking with fewer distractions. The shaking was particularly effective as the boat was being thrashed around by the whirlpool of Charybdis, one of several moments when it really matched the action onscreen. But did we really need to heave up and down every time a soldier marched into a castle? Just because you CAN shake the seats doesn’t mean you SHOULD every time there’s movement in the frame.


	Overall, I enjoyed my first 4DX experience though perhaps a different and simpler film would have been a better choice. I might have liked a few more effects – the smell of pigs, or ripening cheese, perhaps. “We were all praying for forgiveness,” Damon intones in his climactic speech. In the case of 4DX filmgoers, it could be said that after three hours we were all praying mostly for our seats to hold still.

	Matt

	I will kick off my thoughts with a caveat — I couldn’t make it through the entire 4DX experience. I can handle pretty much any roller coaster, but three hours of movement… I’m no Odysseus!

	I began getting nauseous about halfway through, and my stomach couldn’t handle the thought of experiencing the crazy water scenes that I knew were coming closer to the end of the film. But from what I was able to witness for myself, the 4DX experience was a fun, unpredictable mixture of movement happening in your seat and things happening elsewhere in the room, mainly some mist that helped set the scene when Odysseus and his men are out at sea. The audience also gets a necessary break from the constant movement during the more dialogue-heavy scenes, to be fair!

	I don’t know if I would do 4DX again, especially considering the price tag compared to my beloved AMC A-List getting me into Imax and Dolby screenings at no extra cost, which both enhance the film with better sound. For “The Odyssey,” though, it was worth taking the journey, although I wouldn’t have shelled out the $36 for any other film this summer. If you’re looking to check off as many premium formats for Christopher Nolan’s epic as possible, I’d recommend watching the film once before you try out 4DX so you don’t spend your first viewing trying to parse through the very dense plot while trying not to get knocked out of your seat.
	

	Tanya

	As a frequent 4DX filmgoer (and someone who had already seen Nolan’s epic), I was fully prepared for an Odyssey-esque journey of my own. I stocked up on snacks to fuel my nearly three-hour voyage, but, much like Odysseus’ experience, eating proved to be a challenge. I attempted to dip my mozzarella stick in its accompanying sauce during what I had perceived to be a scene that did not require any motion, but our chairs suddenly jolted forward, sending my over-priced mozzarella stick to the floor. Sure, there were plenty of calmer moments to come throughout the film, but my fear of the upcoming action was enough for me to opt to stay hungry.

	When I first saw “The Odyssey” in Imax 70mm, I thought to myself “This couldn’t get any better.” Seeing it in 4DX immediately proved me wrong. You’re not just immersed in the film — you’re living in it. You are battling with Odysseus’ men, dodging strikes from the Laestrygonians, breathing in actual smoke during the Battle of Troy, getting hit in the back in the same spots soldiers were pierced with arrows. You’re experiencing everything they went through — both the men of Greek legend and the actors themselves, which gives a newfound appreciation of the work that went into the film.

	Above all, the best part of this experience is the energy it created in the theater. We had lived through the film together. There were subtle frightened gasps (and even shrieks of terror) during action-packed moments, and we collectively let out sighs of relief when those moments were over. I locked eyes with strangers, sharing laughs while we attempted to not fall out of our seats. We were actively in the film, and when Odysseus finally revealed he had returned to Ithaca, the entire theater erupted into applause. It felt like we had made it home too. Moments like these are hard to replicate.

schema.headline: Shaken, Soaked and Seasick: What It’s Like to Survive ‘The Odyssey’ in 4DX

title: D4vd's Garage Had Possible Blood Stains All Over It, Forensic Criminalist Says
description:
D4vd Preliminary Hearing
                          Possible Bloody Mess in His Garage
body:
D4vd Preliminary Hearing
                          Possible Bloody Mess in His Garage
                          ... Witness Describes Scene
                      
                  
                                                                        Published 
                              July 22, 2026
                            6:11 PM PDT
                                                                            
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            D4vd's garage was possibly covered in blood ... this according to a forensic criminalist who helped process his home.
      
    
            Witness Lauren Wallace -- a criminalist with LAPD's forensic science division -- took the stand during Day 2 of the singer's preliminary hearing for the alleged murder of Celeste Rivas Wednesday ... and said red stains all over the singer's garage tested positive for possible blood.
      
    
            Wallace was there processing the scene in September of 2025 days after Celeste's body was found, and said the team performed BLUESTAR tests -- used to find potential blood evidence -- inside the Hollywood Hills home D4vd was renting.
      

            She testified stains near his Tesla charger and his rowing machine came back positive.
      
    
            Wallace explained the floor was covered by black, rubber interlocking mats which had major damage -- including 7 linear cuts consistent with use of a sharp object -- and said the mats lit up to indicate possible blood during the BLUESTAR tests.
      
    
            The team also processed a stained trash bag, and that also tested positive for possible blood ... and she said the inflatable pool found in the garage smelled like laundry, and had a dryer sheet rolled up inside of it.
      
    
      
        
              
    
    
            Wallace added the mountain of evidence included a green tarp with red stains, disinfectant wipes and 2 chainsaws. However, she said the chainsaws came back negative for blood.
      
    
            Deputy District Attorney Beth Silverman asked Wallace if they'd found a Makita 10-inch chainsaw -- the same one he allegedly ordered May 4, 2025 on Postmates -- and she said no.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                                  
      
    
            D4vd appeared unfazed during the testimony ... just observing the evidence without any real reaction.
      
    
            They went on break in the middle of Wallace's time on the stand, but Celeste's parents -- who'd been in court yesterday and today -- didn't come back when court reconvened.
      
    
            As you know, the singer's been charged with murdering Celeste, whose dismembered body was found last September in the front trunk of his Tesla.
      
      
              
            
              Related articles
            
        
              
          
                          
                Beth Silverman

title: Clavicular Questioned About Injecting Aqualyx in Teen Influencer's Lawsuit
description:
Clavicular Lawsuit
                          Are You Healthcare License-Maxxing???
body:
Clavicular Lawsuit
                          Are You Healthcare License-Maxxing???
                      
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            Clavicular's being questioned about his medical background in a new lawsuit ... courtesy of a teen influencer who claims Clav injected a fat dissolver into her face.
      
    
            The influencer behind the lawsuit is Aleksandra Vasilevna Mendoza ... she claims Clav had sex with her while she was underage and injected her face with Aqualyx -- a substance which helps rid cheeks of fat.
      
    
            In new legal docs, obtained by TMZ, Aleksandra wants to know if Clav has a healthcare license.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                                  
      
    
            Among the questions being lobbed Clavicular's way ... "On or about November 14, 2024, did the Defendant maintain any healthcare license?"
      

            The following question reads ... "Before November 14, 2024, did the Defendant receive any training on how to inject Aqualyx?"
      
    
      
        
              
    
    
            Aleksandra alleges Clav injected Aqualyx in her face during a 2025 live stream ... something she claims she thought he was medically cleared to do.
      
    
            She also says she did not give consent for him to live stream the alleged injections.
      
    
            Clavicular -- through his attorney, Steve Kramer -- has denied all the allegations against him in this lawsuit.

title: Chadwick Boseman's Brothers Want Widow Removed As Estate Administrator Over Alleged Mismanagement
description:
Chadwick Boseman's Brothers
                          We Want His Widow Axed As Estate Administrator!
body:
Chadwick Boseman's Brothers
                          We Want His Widow Axed As Estate Administrator!
                      
                  
                                                                        Published 
                              
                July 22, 2026
                
                            6:30 AM PDT
                                                                      |
                                                                Updated 
                                  July 22, 2026
                                8:31 AM PDT
                                    
              
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            Chadwick Boseman's brothers want his widow to be removed as administrator of his estate nearly 6 years after his death ... and they are petitioning the court to have it done ASAP.
      
    
            Kevin and Derrick Boseman beelined it to a Los Angeles court Friday and submitted legal docs outlining a list of reasons why they believe Chadwick's wife, Taylor Simone Ledward Boseman, has mismanaged the estate and why she should be removed from having control.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            In the docs, obtained by TMZ, Kevin and Derrick claim Taylor left the entire family in the dark about the status of his estate ... despite a 2022 court order that said their parents, Leroy and Carolyn Boseman, were legally entitled to a combined 50% share. The court order gave Taylor control of the other half after Chadwick died without a will in place.
      
    
            But, nearly four years later, Chadwick's brothers allege his widow has not finished distributing the estate's assets and has continued controlling it completely on her own.
      

            Chadwick's estate includes money from movie residuals and royalties, his image and intellectual property rights, investments, insurance policies, bank accounts, and personal belongings ... and his brothers claim his family has no clue about what's going on with any of it. This includes the more than $3 million in assets in various bank accounts.
      
    
            Chadwick's brothers say his widow still needs to transfer a long-term care insurance policy to their mother, properly close and distribute money from Chadwick's various bank accounts, account for SAG-AFTRA residual payments Chadwick earned after his death and collect and distribute unclaimed property belonging to Chadwick.
      
    
            They say this has left Chadwick's parents without funds and insurance they're entitled to, prevented them from pursuing business opportunities involving Chadwick's legacy, caused emotional distress and allowed Taylor to continue to solely profit from Chadwick's name and image.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            And, they say Taylor is well aware of their issues, as they have made numerous pleas to her to set things straight.
      
    
            Kevin and Derrick are now asking the court to hold Taylor in contempt for failing to follow the court's 2022 order and force her to distribute the remaining estate assets as well as provide a complete accounting of the estate within 30 days. They also want to permanently remove her as administrator and appoint attorney Jason Rubin to take over.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            Going forward, Kevin and Derrick ask the court to bar Taylor from signing major contracts involving Chadwick's estate without the family's approval, require her to hand over copies of all contracts related to the estate, and require her to pay the family's attorney's fees and legal costs.
      
    
            As you know, Chadwick died on August 28, 2020 due to complications from colon cancer, a diagnosis he had privately battled for four years.

schema.headline: Chadwick Boseman's Brothers Want Widow Removed As Estate Administrator Over Alleged Mismanagement

title: Carnival Cruise Passenger's Foot Scratching Videos Go Viral
description:
Carnival Cruise
                          Want Some Toe Jam With Your Fork?!? 🤮
body:
Carnival Cruise
                          Want Some Toe Jam With Your Fork?!? 🤮
                      
                  
                                                                        Published 
                              July 22, 2026
                            5:55 PM PDT
                                                                            
      
    
        
                    
                  
      
    
            Diarrhea ain't the only risky biz on cruise ships. One Carnival passenger put her grossest foot forward in an absolute horror show -- a plate full o' food, with one bitch of an itch sprinkled on top!!!
      
    
            The now viral videos show a woman flakin' the hell out as she repeatedly scrapes dry skin from the bottom of her bare foot while, apparently, sitting in a dining area in plain view of her fellow cruisers. Talk about an appetite suppressant!
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                  
      
    
            It's clear from the video she's scratching and eating at a table with other people ... as she casually attacks the bottom of her foot, and the flakes float to the ground like snow.
      

            Somehow, that's not even the grossest part. Moments later, she appears to dust off her fingers and reach back for food off her plate -- perhaps some pizza crust -- and keeps eating without missing a beat.
      
    
        
                    
                  
      
    
            She even called for backup when her fingers weren't getting the job done ... she leveled up and used a metal fork to scratch harder. Mercifully, the footage ends before we can see if the utensil was put back into food service.
      
    
                  
          
                          
                
                                  
      
    
            Carnival's got a sense of humor about the viral ordeal ... joking on Instagram, "We can confirm that forks are intended for food, not foot care." The cruise line said staff quickly stepped in, spoke with the guest and pulled the fork outta circulation.
      
    
            The company also stressed the incident doesn't reflect its standards, noting that while the video's getting plenty of laughs and horrified reactions online, sanitation is something it takes seriously.
      
    
            Take a cruise ... it's endless fungus!!!
      
      
              
            
              Related articles
            
        
              
          
                          
                Internet Culture
              
                          
                Viral
              
                          
                Culture

schema.headline: Carnival Cruise Passenger's Foot Scratching Videos Go Viral
